attributes.stem: A two-year-old girl presents to the General Practice clinic with her father who has concerns about her foot growth. She has met all developmental milestones at the appropriate ages. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Examination of her feet reveals an abnormality of her toes (Image). Her gait is normal and range of movement is not restricted. There are no blisters or calluses present.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management option to recommend?
attributes.explanation:
This child presents with overlapping toes, also known as curly toes, which is a common paediatric condition. Although it may be a point of parental concern, it is generally considered a variant of normal anatomy without underlying pathology. Management is typically conservative and involves measures to separate toes to prevent blister and callus formation.

Broad-toed shoes are beneficial as they may provide additional physical space for toes to spread apart over time. This conservative approach is often sufficient, as spontaneous resolution occurs in approximately 25% of patients.

Toe splints or toe taping are not suitable options in this case. While these techniques have been shown to temporarily improve the condition, the deformity typically recurs after taping or splinting is discontinued. These methods can also be uncomfortable for young children and may interfere with normal foot development.

Surgery is rarely indicated for this problem, especially in a child as young as two years old. Surgical intervention is typically only considered if the condition remains problematic past the age of five years and is causing significant discomfort or functional issues. In most cases, these issues naturally resolve over time without the need for surgical intervention.

Physiotherapy is not the best management option here, given that curly toes are not due to incorrect walking habits. They typically arise due to tight flexor tendons present from birth. The child's gait is described as normal, further indicating that physiotherapy is not necessary.

In summary, the most appropriate management for this child's overlapping toes is to recommend broad-toed shoes. This conservative approach allows for natural resolution while providing comfort and preventing potential complications such as blisters or calluses. Parents should be reassured about the benign nature of this condition and informed about its tendency to improve spontaneously over time.
learningPoints: Overlapping toes often only require conservative management.
Broad-toed shoes can provide space for toes to spread apart over time in cases of overlapping toes.
Surgery is rarely indicated for overlapping toes in young children, as the condition often resolves spontaneously.
references: The Sydney Children's Hospitals Network. Curly Toes [Internet]. Sydney; 2020 [cited 2022 July 12]. Available from: https://www.schn.health.nsw.gov.au/files/factsheets/curly_toes-en.pdf
Hosseinzadeh, P. Curly Toe [Internet]. Paediatric Orthopaedic Society of North America [cited 2022 July 12]. Available from: https://posna.org/Physician-Education/Study-Guide/Curly-Toe
